workflow.name: Triage GitHub issues with OpenAI categorization and embedding-based duplicate detection
workflow.description:
## Who's it for

Open-source maintainers, product teams with public repositories, and any organization receiving a steady stream of GitHub Issues. Ideal for small teams who waste hours per week triaging duplicates and misrouted reports.

## How it works

When a new Issue is opened, a GitHub webhook fires this workflow. It first filters for the "opened" action, then fetches the last 30 Issues from the repository. All Issue texts (new + past) are sent to OpenAI's embeddings API in a single batch call for efficiency. The workflow calculates cosine similarity between the new Issue and every past Issue. If the maximum similarity exceeds 0.85, the new Issue is auto-closed with a comment referencing the original. Otherwise, AI classifies it into one of four categories: bug (adds label + Slack alert to dev team), question (posts FAQ link as a comment), feature (appends to a roadmap Google Sheet), or spam (auto-close with label). AI is used only for classification — the duplicate detection uses deterministic vector math, and every action is rule-based.

## Set up steps

1. Generate a GitHub Personal Access Token with repo scope
2. Create a webhook on your repository pointing to this workflow's URL, subscribing to Issues events
3. Create a Google Sheet named feature_roadmap with columns: date_added, issue_number, title, author, url, status
4. Open Set Configuration and fill in the repo owner, repo name, Sheet ID, Slack channel, and FAQ URL
5. Register GitHub and OpenAI Header Auth credentials and connect Google Sheets and Slack
6. Activate the workflow

## How to customize

Adjust duplicate_threshold for stricter or looser matching, change the embeddings model, or swap Sheets for Notion or Airtable.

workflow.name: Triage GitHub Issues with AI Category and Embedding-Based Duplicate Detection

parameters.body: =👋 Thanks for the report! This looks like a possible duplicate of a recent issue (similarity {{ $json.top_similarity }}). We'll close this one to keep things tidy — please comment on the original issue if this still affects you.

values.content:
You are a GitHub issue triage assistant. Classify the issue into exactly ONE of these categories: "bug", "feature", "question", "spam".

Rules:
- "bug": reports of broken functionality, errors, crashes, regressions.
- "feature": requests for new functionality or enhancements.
- "question": how-to questions, usage help, FAQ-style asks.
- "spam": off-topic, advertising, nonsense, or clearly malicious content.

Respond ONLY with valid JSON in this exact shape (no markdown, no prose):
{"category": "bug|feature|question|spam", "reason": "short explanation, max 200 chars"}

Issue Title: {{ $json.issue_title }}
Issue Body:
{{ $json.issue_body }}

parameters.body:
=👋 Thanks for your question! Many common questions are answered in our FAQ: {{ $('Set Configuration').item.json.faq_url }}

If you can't find your answer there, please reply with more details and a maintainer will help you out.

parameters.content:
# Triage GitHub Issues with AI Category and Embedding-Based Duplicate Detection

When a new Issue is opened, the workflow fetches recent Issues from the same repo, runs batch embeddings to calculate semantic similarity, and auto-closes duplicates with a reference comment. Non-duplicates are classified by AI into one of four categories and routed to the matching action branch.

## How it works
1. Receive a GitHub webhook when a new Issue is opened
2. Filter for the "opened" action only
3. Fetch the last 30 Issues from the repository
4. Send all Issue texts to OpenAI embeddings in a single batch call
5. Calculate cosine similarity between the new Issue and each past Issue
6. If similarity exceeds 0.85, auto-close as duplicate with a reference comment
7. Otherwise, classify the Issue with AI into bug, question, feature, or spam
8. Route to the matching action branch

## Categories
- Bug: Add label and send Slack alert to dev team
- Question: Post FAQ link as a comment
- Feature: Append to roadmap Google Sheet
- Spam: Auto-close with spam label
- Duplicate: Auto-close with reference to the original Issue

## Setup steps
1. Generate a GitHub Personal Access Token with repo scope
2. Create a webhook on your repository subscribing to Issues events
3. Create a Google Sheet named feature_roadmap with columns: date_added, issue_number, title, author, url, status
4. Open Set Configuration and fill in the repo owner, repo name, Sheet ID, Slack channel, and FAQ URL
5. Connect GitHub, OpenAI, Google Sheets, and Slack credentials
6. Activate the workflow

## 1. Trigger & Filter

GitHub webhook receives Issue events → load central configuration (owner/repo, thresholds, FAQ URL, Slack channel, Roadmap sheet) → keep only `opened` actions and extract the issue number / title / body for downstream use.

## 2. Fetch & Embed

Pull the last 30 Issues from the same repo, combine titles + bodies, and send them all to the OpenAI embeddings API in a single batch request for cost & latency efficiency.

## 3. Similarity & Dedup

Compute cosine similarity between the new Issue and every fetched Issue. If the top score exceeds `duplicate_threshold` (default 0.85) → route to the Duplicate branch, otherwise continue to AI classification.

## 4. Classify & Dispatch

GPT-4o-mini labels the Issue as one of bug / feature / question / spam (deterministic JSON). A Switch node fans out to five side effects: duplicate handling, bug alerting, FAQ reply, roadmap logging, and spam closure.

### 🔁 Duplicate → Comment
Polite "possible duplicate" comment posted on the new Issue with the similarity score.

### 🔒 Duplicate → Close
Closes the Issue and applies the `duplicate` label.

### 🐛 Bug → Label
Adds the `bug` + `triage:auto` labels to the Issue.

### 📣 Bug → Slack Alert
Pings the configured dev Slack channel with the issue link.

### ❓ Question → FAQ Reply
Auto-replies with a link to the FAQ documentation.

### ✨ Feature → Roadmap
Appends the request to a Google Sheets roadmap backlog.

### 🚫 Spam → Close
Closes the Issue immediately and applies the `spam` label.

user.bio: Solo automation builder and content creator. I help busy solopreneurs eliminate repetitive tasks with n8n and AI — so the work runs itself.

data.details:
The IF node can be used to implement binary conditional logic in your workflow. You can set up one-to-many conditions to evaluate each item of data being inputted into the node. That data will either evaluate to TRUE or FALSE and route out of the node accordingly.

This node has multiple types of conditions: Bool, String, Number, and Date & Time.
